I updated the outdated docstring to the following:

> This function returns two lists as a tuple. The first list has the length of `|step_output|`, in which the i-th element are all i-th elements of `step_output` from all steps, stacked along axis 0. The second list has the length of `|loop_vars|`, which represents final states of loop variables.

Currently we don't have dynamic shape inference, so could not support TF-like dynamic-sized, per-time-step `TensorArray`. So we split our requirement into two parts: 1. per-time-step array with `max_iteration`; 2. loop variables which are of the same shape through the loop.

This is the block that made me think this change was worth making.  If something inside the block fails, obj._cross_validation_lock will be stuck on `True`.  

I think this happens to be harmless here, because we're assuming that we're being called from `obj.__new__`, which means an uncaught exception will prevent a live instance to the invalid object from ever existing, but repeating this pattern elsewhere would result in hard-to-find bugs where the relative ordering of validators would cause very different behaviors.

This resolution is now happening before the cache checks.  This results in an unnecessary requirement that the entire chain of assumed profiles is not expired.

However, these intermediate results aren't actually used if a subsequent role *is* cached.  For example, consider the role chain `A->B->C`.  Let's say C  and B are expired in the cache, but A's entry in `~/.aws/cli/cache` is up to date.  This means the sequence of calls you'll make is:

* `AssumeRole(C)`
* `AssumeRole(B)`
* Used cache credentials from A

It seems like the ideal behavior would be if we're trying to assume the role in profile `A`, as long as it's cache entry is up to date we should just use it.

You can test this behavior by just deleting the cache file for `B` and `C` in `~/.aws/cli/cache`

> should we raise an exception in gsobject.drawKImage() if both add_to_image and recenter are True? Seems like it might be surprising behavior to do both.

Why would it be surprising?  `recenter=True` is the default, and I suspect `recenter=False` would be a rare use case.  I mostly added that option because `_drawKImage` doesn't recenter, so I wanted to have a place to describe what that meant, and then the doc string for `_drawKImage` can say it is equivalent to `drawKImage(..., recenter=False)`.

But it seems to me that the normal use case for `add_to_image=True` would be something like:
```
obj1.drawKImage(im)
obj2.drawKImage(im, add_to_image=True)
```
That should probably behave sensibly without a warning.

Hi,

first: I would take all ttests of scipy.stats in neuroM (middleterm), depending on how much work it is to integrate them.

second: which default? Depends on which is the most frequent case the customers have. I would take either the  scipy.stats.ttest_ind(a, b, axis=0, equal_var=True)  if the the distributions are more or less unimodal and symmetric or the mannwhitneyu otherwise (if they are highly skewed and obviously non-normal, for example). All the above tests work for continuous distributions ONLY. Special attention should be paid to equal_var=True or False depending on what one has. It switches between two different tests.

The reason not to make the kstest the default is that it is sensitive to all kinds of shape differences of the distributions. The tests mentioned above focus on differences in location parameters like the means (ttest) or medians (mannwhittneyu).

Another caution point is the wether the two samples are correlated (“related" in the scipy.stats language) or not. The corresponding versions of the tests are: scipy.stats.ttest_rel(a, b, axis=0)  for the ttest_ind and for the mannwhitneyu is the wilcoxon.

ATTENTION!!! the following statement is wrong. It’s the other way around: small p-values point to significantly different distributions. More specific: if pval < 0.05 then we reject the null hypothesis that the two distributions are the same. If pval>=0.05 we cannot reject the null, which DOES NOT MEAN THAT THE TWO DATA SETS COME FROM THE SAME DISTRIBUTION. What I say in this case is that: there is no evidence for significant difference, thus I do not undertake any action (to modify the system, for example).

I hope that helps, have fun,

nancy

> +            Small numbers define high probability the data come from\

> + same dataset.

This won't work. We want max_bytes to be a strict upper limit here, so that users cannot exceed message size limits due to our batching. For example, if a user sends a 1 byte message and a 10 MB message, and the limit is set to the max of 10 MB, the messages should be sent separately. If sent together they will fail at CPS since they are greater than 10 MB. What we've done in other languages is send the batch and make the message that was published the first message of a new batch.

You also don't handle large messages here. For example if the limit is 10 KB, and we have a batch of 5 KB and a user publishes 15 KB, we should publish them both, since we've exceeded the limit we want to publish the batch, and we want to send the large message directly since it already exceeds the limit on its own.

Specifically, I'm worried that baking the concept of 'role' into into the plugin API makes it much harder to evolve that concept later. We've already seen roles evolved a bit (superusers, device permissions, and class scoped coaches).

More generally, trying to schematize these items I think could could impede evolution of our design by locking us into the way we happen to do things now. For example, what if we decided that we wanted to add a number badge (like unread notifications, points) or other more complex interactions (pop-ups, etc)?

That's why I was thinking it would be good if plugins could drop in their own menu item component implementations, almost slot-style. These could either re-use a core `<k-side-nav-item>` implementation, or build their own from scratch. Similarly, visibility logic could depend on any core or plugin state...

Instead of writing a class that delegates to a web3 contract instance, I think it might be better to simply subclass the web3 contract class to add the necessary business logic.

```python
from web3.contract import Contract

class VMC(Contract):
    abi = <the-vmc-abi>
    bytecode = <the-vmc-bytecode>

    def sample(self, shard_id, sender_addr=None, gas=TX_GAS):
        txn_params = self.mk_txn_params(sender=sender, gas=gas)
        address_as_hex = self.call(tx_params).sample(shard_id)
        return decode_hex(address_in_hex)
```

I think this results in a cleaner and easier to read API, removing the initialization code.  Thoughts?

Counter-point: the analogous calls, like `estimate_gas_transaction` sound more like verbs, too, so `build` is a bit more consistent with those. On that point, I'd argue that the best solution would be to change them all to nouns, but maybe it's less obvious what the other nouns should be, like: `gas_estimate_transaction`?

I understand your viewpoint. The current reactive framework is built in such a way that you have to know what Juju is doing in order to use reactive correctly. Hiding hooks in the current implementation will only cause more confusion about why stuff is behaving in a certain way. If we can explain every behavior using reactive concepts, then, but only then, is it safe to hide the hooks.

I think the current "external events" concept explains the behavior correctly, using concepts that are more native to reactive.

Again, the advantage of explaining everything in reactive is that the learning curve for new users will be a lot smaller, new users will experience a lot less unexpected behavior, and we pave the way to put the reactive framework in places/situations where hooks don't exist.

I think the only reason you should use `is None` is for readability; there should be one way to read it.  Other than that, `None == None` works perfectly fine.

As an example, consider flipping a boolean mask in numpy: `myarray == False` obviously can't be replaced with `myarray is False` (it can with `myarray *-1` but that's not the point).  So the rule isn't universal to begin with.  And not clear that `cr == CROP_NONE` reads any worse than `cr is CROP_NONE`.
